title-group.article-title: Longitudinal relationships between changes in post-COVID mental fatigue and neuropsychiatric symptoms across distinct recovery trajectories

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/02tbvhh96grid.452438.c0000 0004 1760 8119Department of Nuclear Medicine, The First Affiliated Hospital of Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, Shaanxi Province China

To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Persistent fatigue, often accompanied by neuropsychiatric symptoms (e.g., cognitive impairment, sleep disorders, depression, anxiety, and PTSD), commonly referred to as “long COVID”. Fatigue is multidimensional, comprising physical, psychological, and situational components. However, the relationships between these fatigue dimensions and co-occurring neuropsychiatric symptoms remain unclear. This study aimed to (1) examine the time-varying characteristics of post-COVID fatigue and (2) determine whether specific fatigue dimensions uniquely affect neuropsychiatric symptoms in long COVID.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We analyzed neuropsychiatric assessments from 233 individuals at 1 and 3 months post-infection using Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA). Participants were categorized into four groups based on fatigue recovery trajectories: persistent fatigue, alleviated fatigue, new-onset fatigue, and no fatigue. Longitudinal CCA assessed relationships between changes in fatigue dimensions and cognitive impairments (memory, executive function) and psychiatric symptoms (anxiety, depression, PTSD, and sleep disturbances) from the acute to chronic phase.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Physical fatigue was dominant in the acute phase, while mental fatigue became more prominent at 3 months. Across both phases, all fatigue dimensions were strongly associated with depression, anxiety, PTSD, and sleep disturbances, with mental fatigue showing the strongest impact (canonical loadings: ρ = − 0.875 at 1 month; ρ = − 0.914 at 3 months). In the persistent fatigue group, mental fatigue was closely linked to anxiety, depression, and cognitive impairments, including recognition and short-term recall (ρ = − 0.677). No significant longitudinal associations were observed in the alleviated, new-onset, or no-fatigue groups.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Mental fatigue plays a central role in the chronic phase of recovery, significantly impacting cognitive and psychiatric health in individuals with persistent fatigue. Targeted interventions addressing mental fatigue are essential for improving long COVID outcomes.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s10787-025-01860-5.

funding-source.institution: the national natural science foundation of China

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: A substantial proportion of individuals recovering from mild COVID-19 infections are challenged by persistent neuropsychiatric symptoms (e.g. fatigue, cognitive impairment, sleep disorders, depression, anxiety and post-traumatic stress disorder), commonly referred to as “long COVID” (The 2021). Among these symptoms, fatigue is the most prevalent and debilitating (Ceban et al. 2022; Rogers et al. 2020). Increasing evidence highlights the profound impact of fatigue on health-related quality of life (HRQoL), affecting not only physical function but also mental and social well-being (Beyer et al. 2023; Calabria et al. 2022). Notably, fatigue is a multidimensional phenomenon, encompassing physical, psychological, motivational, situational, and activity-related dimensions (Campos et al. 2022; Mamyrbaev et al. 2023). However, the relationship between these dimensions of post-COVID fatigue and the co-occurring neuropsychiatric symptoms remains unclear, complicating efforts to develop effective interventions for individuals with long COVID (Koc et al. 2022).

Mental fatigue, characterized by burnout and mental exhaustion, is a psychobiological state resulting from prolonged periods of intense cognitive activity (de Raaf et al. 2013; Jonasson et al. 2018), whereas physical fatigue typically manifests as diminished physical performance (Huang et al. 2021). Following the global COVID-19 pandemic, individuals recovering from COVID-19 in the early phase (within 37 days post-infection) have shown more severe and prolonged impairments in skeletal muscle strength and physical performance compared to those recovering from other respiratory viral infections (Paneroni et al. 2021). Physical fatigue is frequently accompanied by musculoskeletal pain (Tuzun et al. 2021). However, mental fatigue, which can persist for four to eight months post-infection, presents distinct challenges (Calabria et al. 2022; Morin et al. 2021; Van Herck et al. 2021). It is associated with prolonged declines in response inhibition, information processing, and concentration, ultimately leading to reduced productivity and overall cognitive decline (Lambregts et al. 2023; Wang et al. 2022a, b). A recent cross-sectional study of 136 COVID-19 individuals with cognitive complaints at 8 months post-infection identified depressive symptoms and executive dysfunction as predictors of the psychosocial component of fatigue (Calabria et al. 2022). However, cross-sectional studies fail to capture the dynamic changes in post-COVID fatigue dimensions. Furthermore, limited neuropsychiatric assessments hinder a comprehensive understanding of how different fatigue dimensions impact co-occurring neurological and psychiatric symptoms, particularly as these symptoms progress from the acute phase to long COVID manifestations.

There has been growing attention to the considerable variability in the presentation of long COVID (Huang et al. 2022; Townsend et al. 2021). A meta-analysis revealed that acute fatigue typically diminishes during the course of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection, while certain subpopulations continue to experience prolonged symptoms (Ceban et al. 2022). The impact of fatigue on functional and psychological recovery may play a crucial role in the development and persistence of these symptoms (Chaudhuri & Behan 2004; Van Cutsem et al. 2017). In particular, we anticipate that individuals with divergent recovery trajectories—from the acute phase to three months after infection—will exhibit different patterns of association between changes in fatigue dimensions and co-occurring neuropsychiatric symptoms. These subpopulations include those with persistent fatigue, alleviated fatigue, new-onset fatigue, and no fatigue (Bower et al. 2018). Investigating these longitudinal trajectories is essential for facilitating early interventions and enhancing management strategies for long COVID.

To address these issues, we conducted comprehensive neuropsychiatric assessments and employed Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA), a multivariate method well-suited for analyzing multiple sets of variables with varying levels of specificity (Black et al. 2018; Meng et al. 2022). CCA was applied to investigate the cross-sectional relationships between the time-varying characteristics of post-COVID fatigue and co-occurring cognitive impairments, specifically memory and executive function, as well as psychiatric sequelae, including anxiety, depression, PTSD, and sleep disturbances, during both the acute phase and at 3 months post-infection. Additionally, longitudinal CCA analysis was performed to evaluate long-term changes in fatigue characteristics and their associations with shifts in neuropsychiatric sequelae, focusing on the critical 4- to 12-week post-COVID period.

sec.title: Study design and subject recruitment
sec.p: This study adopted a prospective methodological design and was conducted across nine hospitals in China between January and April 2023. The Ethics Committee of the First Affiliated Hospital of Xi’an Jiaotong University approved this protocol, and voluntary written informed consent was obtained from all participants.

Individuals aged 18–65 years who were diagnosed with mild COVID-19 based on positive nasopharyngeal swab reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and/or serology (anti-SARS-CoV2 IgM or IgG) infection were enrolled as participants. Individuals were excluded if they had a history of neurological and/or psychiatric illness. Finally, a total of 233 individuals were recruited in the acute phase cohort and underwent neuropsychiatric assessment within 30 days of symptom onset (acute phase, 23.2 ± 5.6 days). All individuals were then followed up with the same neuropsychiatric assessment 3 months after COVID-19.

sec.title: Assessment of fatigue
sec.p: The Multidimensional Fatigue Inventory-20 (MFI-20) consists of 20 items designed to assess five dimensions of fatigue: general fatigue, physical fatigue, reduced motivation, reduced activity, and mental fatigue (Mamyrbaev et al. 2023; Smets et al. 1995). Each dimension includes four items rated on a five-point scale: 1 (not at all), 2 (a little bit), 3 (moderately), 4 (quite a bit), and 5 (extremely). Total scores range from 20 to 100, with higher scores indicating greater fatigue severity. A score of ≥ 12 on any dimension indicates fatigue syndrome. The original MFI-20 has demonstrated high validity and acceptable reliability, with a Cronbach’s alpha of 0.84.

sec.title: Assessment of other co-occurring neuropsychiatric symptoms
sec.p: To comprehensively assess co-occurring neuropsychiatric symptoms, the following scales were administered: (1) the Impact of Events Scale-Revised (IES-R) for assessing PTSD (Creamer et al. 2003); (2) the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) and the Beck Anxiety Inventory (BAI) for evaluating the severity of depression and anxiety, respectively (Beck et al. 1961, 1988); and (3) the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) for measuring sleep disturbances (Buysse et al. 1989). Neurological symptoms were assessed using: (4) the Auditory Verbal Learning Test-Hua Shan version (AVLT-H) to evaluate immediate recall, short-term delayed recall, long-term delayed recall, and recognition (Wang et al. 2022a, b); and (5) the Trail-Making Test Parts A (TMT-A) and B (TMT-B) to assess cognitive processing speed (Llinàs-Reglà et al. 2017). A detailed description of these scales is provided in the Supplemental Information.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All analyses were conducted using SPSS version 27.0, with p < 0.05 considered statistically significant. The Kolmogorov–Smirnov test assessed the normality of quantitative variables. Normally distributed variables were reported as means (SD), non-normally distributed variables as medians (25th–75th percentiles), and categorical variables as counts (%).

Paired t-tests compared neuropsychiatric assessments between the acute phase and the 3-month follow-up. Pearson correlation analysis explored simple associations between fatigue dimensions and neuropsychiatric symptoms across the two phases.

Four longitudinal fatigue trajectories were defined based on MFI measurements: (1) Persistent fatigue group: Fatigue at both the acute phase and 3 months; (2) Alleviated fatigue group: Fatigue in the acute phase but not at 3 months; (3) Newly-onset fatigue group: No fatigue in the acute phase but fatigue at 3 months; (4) No fatigue group: No fatigue in either phase. Additionally, we introduced the symbol Δ (representing the value at 3 months post-infection minus the value at 1 month) to quantify the longitudinal changes in neuropsychiatric symptoms.

Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) is a multivariate method used to examine relationships between two sets of correlated variables. In this study, CCA explored the linear associations between fatigue dimensions and neuropsychiatric symptoms, with canonical correlation coefficients quantifying the strength of these associations and canonical loadings (ρ) indicating each variable’s contribution.

Two cross-sectional CCA analyses were conducted for the acute phase and 3 months post-infection to identify associations between five fatigue dimensions (general, physical, mental, reduced activities, reduced motivation from the MFI) and ten neuropsychiatric symptoms (PTSD, TMT-A, TMT-B, immediate recall, short-term delayed recall, long-term delayed recall, recognition, anxiety, depression, sleep disturbances). A longitudinal CCA was then performed to assess relationships between changes in fatigue dimensions and symptoms across four fatigue trajectory groups.

sec.title: Performance of demographic and neuropsychiatric assessments across two post-COVID infection phases
sec.p:
Among the 233 participants with mild COVID-19 infection within 1 month (50.6% female, median age 39.4 ± 10.5 years), all individuals completed neuropsychiatric follow-ups at 3 months post-infection (Table 1). Compared with the acute phase, fatigue, anxiety, and depression severity decreased by 3 months, and improvements were observed in executive function and working memory scores. However, recognition function declined, and symptoms of PTSD and sleep disturbances showed no significant improvement at the 3-month follow-up. Table 1
Demographic characteristics and performance on neuropsychiatric assessments for participants with mild COVID-19 during the acute phase and at 3 months post-infection
Acute phase (n = 233)3 months post-infection (n = 233)P value
Demographic characteristics
Age, years39.4 ± 10.539.4 ± 10.5NA
Female118 (50.6%)118 (50.6%)NA
BMI[kg/m2]24.0 ± 3.223.9 ± 3.60.759
Education years15.6 ± 3.616.0 ± 3.10.245
Neurological assessments
MFI total scores51.53 ± 16.2244.92 ± 14.66 < 0.001
General fatigue11.99 ± 4.049.90 ± 3.49 < 0.001
Physical fatigue10.55 ± 4.088.68 ± 3.50 < 0.001
Mental fatigue9.76 ± 4.058.95 ± 3.830.003
Reduced activities10.13 ± 3.579.12 ± 3.28 < 0.001
Reduced motivation9.10 ± 3.538.28 ± 3.21 < 0.001
TMT-A44.44 ± 18.1540.24 ± 15.280.037
TMT-B88.64 ± 46.4889.09 ± 44.410.073
Immediate recall24.73 ± 6.2526.83 ± 5.98 < 0.001
Short-term delayed recall9.03 ± 2.409.52 ± 2.100.002
Long-term delayed recall8.93 ± 2.469.31 ± 2.300.019
Recognition21.48 ± 3.4920.10 ± 5.76 < 0.001
Psychiatric assessments
IES-R12.61 ± 14.0013.43 ± 13.760.384
BAI5.98 ± 6.873.88 ± 5.46 < 0.001
BDI7.11 ± 7.154.90 ± 6.65 < 0.001
PSQI7.02 ± 3.976.55 ± 3.290.075
BMI body mass index
avalues shown are Mean ± SD, unless otherwise noted

Notably, 68.2% and 50.2% of individuals experienced fatigue during the acute phase and 3 months after infection, respectively. The scores on the five dimensions of fatigue decreased over time, but the dominant characteristics were different. During the acute phase, general fatigue, physical fatigue, and reduced activities were the most prominent dimensions. By 3 months post-infection, general fatigue, reduced activities, and mental fatigue became the dominant characteristics.

sec.title: Cross-sectional univariate analysis of fatigue dimensions and neuropsychiatric symptoms across two post-infection phases
sec.p: For Pearson correlation analyses of cross-sectional phenotypes, results showed that all five fatigue dimensions were positively associated with PTSD, anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances, with the strongest correlation between mental fatigue and depression (r = 0.506, p < 0.001). Additionally, mental fatigue, reduced activity, and reduced motivation were negatively correlated with working memory and executive function, with the strongest correlation between reduced motivation and TMT-B (r = 0.198, p < 0.001) (Supplemental Fig. 1a). A similar pattern was observed at 3 months post-infection. (Supplemental Fig. 1b).

sec.title: Cross-sectional multivariate analysis of fatigue dimensions and neuropsychiatric symptoms across two post-infection phases
sec.p: Through multivariate canonical correlation analysis (CCA), five pairs of canonical variables were extracted for each post-infection phase. The observed cross-sectional univariate associations were effectively captured by the first canonical variate, which explained 95.1% and 95.9% of the shared variance between the five dimensions of fatigue and the ten neuropsychiatric assessments in each phase. The canonical correlation coefficients were r = 0.651 for the acute phase and r = 0.734 for the 3-month post-infection phase, both with p < 0.001.

In the acute phase, fatigue was primarily represented by general fatigue (ρ = − 0.876), mental fatigue (ρ = − 0.875), physical fatigue (ρ = − 0.832), reduced motivation (ρ = − 0.813), and reduced activity (ρ = − 0.796), while the neuropsychiatric component was dominated by depression (ρ = − 0.860), anxiety (ρ = − 0.833), PTSD (ρ = − 0.767), sleep disturbance (ρ = − 0.517), executive function (ρ = − 0.255), and memory measures (ρ = 0.236 for immediate recall, ρ = 0.208 for short-term delayed memory). The strongest associations were between fatigue and depression, anxiety, PTSD, and sleep disturbance. General and mental fatigue were most strongly linked to the exacerbation of these symptoms (Fig. 1a).Fig. 1
Cross-sectional multivariate analysis of fatigue dimensions and neurological and neuropsychiatric symptoms at two post-infection phases (a: acute phase, b: 3 months post-infection). Bars extending inside the circle indicate negative canonical loadings, while those outside the circle indicate positive canonical loadings. The bar length represents the absolute value of the canonical loadings, and variables with loadings greater than 0.3 are marked with an asterisk (*)

At 3 months post-infection, the fatigue component was again dominated by mental fatigue (ρ = − 0.914), general fatigue (ρ = − 0.895), and physical fatigue (ρ = − 0.870), with reduced motivation (ρ = − 0.762) and reduced activity (ρ = − 0.690) also contributing. The neuropsychiatric component was primarily represented by depression (ρ = − 0.863), anxiety (ρ = − 0.815), sleep disturbance (ρ = − 0.754), PTSD (ρ = − 0.736), and memory measures (ρ = 0.249 for short-term delayed recall, ρ = 0.235 for long-term delayed memory). The joint multivariate association was most strongly influenced by depression, anxiety, sleep disturbance, and PTSD. Mental fatigue showed the strongest correlation with the exacerbation of these symptoms (Fig. 1b).

sec.title: Performance of neuropsychiatric symptoms across the four longitudinal fatigue trajectory groups
sec.p:
Among the 159 individuals with acute-phase fatigue, over half reported persistent fatigue at 3 months. In the acute phase, those with persistent fatigue had significantly higher scores in fatigue, PTSD, anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances compared to those without persistent fatigue. This group also experienced more pronounced physical and mental fatigue. At 3 months, individuals with persistent fatigue showed elevated scores across all fatigue dimensions, along with increased psychiatric symptoms (PTSD, anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances) and neurological impairments, including deficits in short-term delayed recall and TMT-A performance (Table 2). Table 2
Performance on neuropsychiatric assessments for participants with persistent fatigue and with alleviated fatigue during the acute phase and at 3 months post-infection
Acute phase3 months post-infection
The alleviated fatigue group (n = 68)The persistent fatigue group (n = 91)P valueThe alleviated fatigue group (n = 68)The persistent fatigue group (n = 91)P value
MFI total scores56.10 ± 12.0260.69 ± 11.510.01634.32 ± 9.0357.19 ± 9.34 < 0.001
General fatigue13.41 ± 2.8514.09 ± 3.080.1597.72 ± 2.3412.56 ± 2.59 < 0.001
Physical fatigue11.72 ± 3.3012.59 ± 3.380.0156.57 ± 2.1411.20 ± 3.18 < 0.001
Mental fatigue10.28 ± 3.6811.85 ± 3.520.0076.59 ± 2.3111.96 ± 3.13 < 0.001
Reduced activities11.03 ± 3.1211.47 ± 3.220.3856.88 ± 2.4011.27 ± 2.56 < 0.001
Reduced motivation9.66 ± 3.6010.69 ± 2.930.0486.56 ± 2.4010.20 ± 2.71 < 0.001
TMT-A42.48 ± 12.6540.72 ± 17.960.50943.59 ± 16.9038.24 ± 15.800.042
TMT-B91.51 ± 42.9389.95 ± 50.210.83792.69 ± 47.6090.27 ± 49.510.756
Immediate recall25.31 ± 6.0923.63 ± 6.940.11327.72 ± 6.1326.15 ± 6.220.283
Short-term delayed recall9.31 ± 2.298.76 ± 2.560.1639.9 ± 1.999.08 ± 2.400.023
Long-term delayed recall9.35 ± 2.348.53 ± 2.600.0419.56 ± 2.198.97 ± 2.630.134
Recognition22.19 ± 3.0121.19 ± 3.730.07020.40 ± 5.6219.99 ± 5.840.658
IES-R10.63 ± 12.0719.15 ± 15.79 < 0.0019.66 ± 10.9719.99 ± 15.01 < 0.001
BAI5.71 ± 7.698.47 ± 7.390.0231.97 ± 2.746.44 ± 6.63 < 0.001
BDI6.10 ± 5.8210.76 ± 8.13 < 0.0012.47 ± 3.968.33 ± 7.75 < 0.001
PSQI6.72 ± 3.538.27 ± 4.010.0125.53 ± 2.627.86 ± 3.60 < 0.001

Among the 74 participants without acute-phase fatigue, 49 remained fatigue-free, while 25 developed new-onset fatigue. In the acute phase, the new-onset group had higher scores for reduced activity and lower immediate and long-term delayed recall compared to the fatigue-free group. At 3 months, they exhibited significantly higher fatigue scores, increased neuropsychiatric symptoms (PTSD, anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances), and impairments in long-term delayed recall (Table 3). Table 3
Performance on neuropsychiatric assessments for participants with no fatigue throughout and with the new onset fatigue during the acute phase and at 3 months post-infection
Acute phase3 months post-infection

Recognition21.47 ± 3.1120.76 ± 4.540.45220.60 ± 5.5818.90 ± 6.170.264
IES-R4.98 ± 6.797.05 ± 11.540.3525.52 ± 7.6613.71 ± 12.740.002
BAI2.18 ± 2.413.76 ± 2.790.0201.40 ± 2.634.00 ± 5.840.013
BDI2.71 ± 3.543.90 ± 3.950.2171.46 ± 2.744.43 ± 5.820.005
PSQI5.31 ± 3.635.90 ± 3.360.5205.08 ± 2.367.24 ± 3.350.003

sec.title: Longitudinal univariate analysis of fatigue dimensions and neuropsychiatric symptoms across the four longitudinal fatigue trajectory groups
sec.p: For Pearson correlation analyses of longitudinal phenotypes, persistent fatigue group showed that increases in general, mental, and physical fatigue were mildly associated with changes in anxiety and depression, with the strongest correlation observed between mental fatigue and depression (r = 0.457, p = 0.002). Fatigue dimensions were also slightly associated with declines in working memory and executive function, particularly between general fatigue and short-term delayed recall (r = 0.220, p = 0.022; Supplemental Fig. 2b).

In alleviated fatigue group, increases in all fatigue dimensions were mildly to moderately associated with changes in anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances, with mental fatigue showing the strongest association with depression (r = 0.316, p < 0.001). No associations were observed with working memory or executive function (Supplemental Fig. 2a).

In no fatigue group, increases in physical fatigue were mildly correlated with changes in depression, while changes in reduced activity and reduced motivation were associated with short-term delayed recall (Supplemental Fig. 3a).

In new-onset fatigue group, increases in reduced activity were moderately associated with declines in executive function (TMT-A), while reduced motivation was correlated with increases in depression (Supplemental Fig. 3b).

sec.title: Longitudinal multivariate analysis of fatigue dimensions and neuropsychiatric symptoms across the four longitudinal fatigue trajectory groups
sec.p:
Through the multivariate analysis with CCA, five pairs of canonical variables were extracted from persistent fatigue group. The first pairs of canonical variables showed significant (P = 0.010), and the canonical correlation coefficient were 0.624, which was included in the follow-up analysis. The top of fatigue component was well represented by the change of general fatigue (canonical loadings ρ = − 0.733), mental fatigue (ρ = − 0.677), physical fatigue (ρ = − 0.432), and reduced activity (ρ = 0.420), while the neuropsychiatric component was most represented by anxiety (ρ = − 0.673), depression (ρ = − 0.639), recognition (ρ = 0.351), short-term delayed memory (ρ = 0.305), sleep disturbance (ρ = − 0.299) and executive function (TMT-B, ρ = − 0.262). The multivariate association between changes in fatigue characteristics and neuropsychiatric symptoms over time was strongest for changes in anxiety, followed by depression and memory problems. Longitudinal worsening of general and mental fatigue was associated with worsening of anxiety, depression and memory problems over time (Fig. 2).Fig. 2
Longitudinal multivariate analysis of fatigue dimensions and neurological and neuropsychiatric symptoms in individuals experiencing persistent fatigue. Bars extending inside the circle indicate negative canonical loadings, while those outside the circle indicate positive canonical loadings. The bar length represents the absolute value of the canonical loadings, and variables with loadings greater than 0.3 are marked with an asterisk (*)
However, no pairs of canonical variables reached statistical significance in the alleviated fatigue group, the no-fatigue group, or the new-onset fatigue group (all p > 0.05).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: To our knowledge, this is the first study to longitudinally investigate the dynamic changes in fatigue dimensions and their relationships with neuropsychiatric symptoms in a well-defined cohort of Chinese individuals with mild COVID-19. Our findings indicate that physical fatigue was more prominent in the acute phase, whereas mental fatigue became increasingly dominant over the subsequent three months. Cross-sectional multivariate analyses revealed that all fatigue dimensions—particularly mental fatigue—were most strongly associated with depression, anxiety, PTSD, and sleep disturbances during both recovery phases. These associations were further corroborated by longitudinal analyses, which showed that individuals with persistent fatigue experienced stronger correlations between changes in general and mental fatigue and variations in anxiety, depression, and cognitive deficits, particularly in recognition and short-term delayed recall. By contrast, no significant longitudinal associations were observed in the alleviated fatigue, new-onset fatigue, or no-fatigue groups.

At the end of 2022, an outbreak of the Omicron variant of COVID-19 occurred in China. Our follow-up cohort study demonstrated a significant reduction in the severity of most post-COVID-19 neuropsychiatric symptoms over time. This improvement may be attributed to a natural or vaccine-induced boost in immunity, or to the reduced virulence and pathogenicity of the Omicron variant (Mattiuzzi & Lippi 2023; Poole-Wright et al. 2023; Schou et al. 2021). However, the persistent decline in recognition scores, along with unresolved PTSD and sleep disturbances, indicates that even mild COVID-19 infections can have a lasting medium-term impact on affected individuals, aligning with global observations of other long-term COVID-19 sequelae (Lorenzo et al. 2023; Townsend et al. 2020).

Building on previous studies highlighting fatigue as the most common complaint among COVID-19 individuals, regardless of disease severity (Townsend et al. 2020), we found that fatigue remained a prominent symptom both during the acute phase and three months post-infection, with more than half of individuals in this Chinese cohort experiencing it at both stages. Fatigue is often regarded as a unidimensional symptom in other clinical conditions (Bower 2014). Whether it occurs in the acute phase or three months after COVID-19, the primary feature of fatigue remains general fatigue, encompassing both physical and psychological aspects, underscoring the complex and multidimensional nature of post-COVID fatigue. More specifically, physical fatigue is more pronounced in the acute phase (Paneroni et al. 2021), likely due to the physiological strain and physical exertion experienced by individuals. In contrast, mental fatigue became more significant three months post-infection, possibly linked to long-term psychological distress, emotional challenges, and cognitive decline (Calabria et al. 2022). Therefore, managing fatigue effectively requires stage-specific approaches. During the early stages of infection, ensuring adequate rest, sleep, and nutrition is essential (Mayer et al. 2021). In later stages, interventions such as appropriate physical activity, psychological support, and mindfulness training may help alleviate persistent fatigue (Oliveira et al. 2024; Tosato et al. 2022).

Our results corroborate prior research reporting strong associations between fatigue severity and depression or anxiety in emerging infectious diseases. For instance, Manning et al. identified stress as a mediator exacerbating depression and anxiety in individuals with COVID-19, while Calabria et al. showed that depressive symptoms predicted the psychosocial aspects of fatigue (Calabria et al. 2022; Manning et al. 2022). Our cross-sectional CCA analysis further emphasized the central role of mental fatigue in linking fatigue to neuropsychiatric symptoms. This was particularly evident three months post-infection, when mental fatigue appeared to exacerbate cognitive and emotional challenges. These findings align with previous studies highlighting limited improvements in emotional and cognitive health despite physiotherapy management, suggesting that unresolved fatigue—especially mental fatigue—remains a critical barrier to recovery (Mayer et al. 2021).

Long COVID, defined as symptoms persisting beyond four weeks post-infection, was evident in over half of our cohort, who reported persistent fatigue three months post-infection. This high prevalence mirrors global reports (Ceban et al. 2022). Individuals with persistent fatigue exhibited significantly higher rates of depression, anxiety, sleep disturbances, PTSD, and cognitive deficits compared to those whose fatigue resolved. Notably, individuals with new-onset fatigue also demonstrated elevated depression scores and cognitive impairments in early phase post-infection, reinforcing the close links between persistent or emerging fatigue and adverse psychological and cognitive health outcomes.

Our longitudinal multivariate analysis using CCA revealed distinct fatigue trajectories and their associations with cognitive decline, particularly in memory and executive function, in individuals with persistent fatigue. The combined burden of mental fatigue and cognitive dysfunction significantly impacts occupational productivity (Davis et al. 2021). The declines observed underscore the urgent need for interventions addressing the psychological dimensions of fatigue from both clinical and occupational health perspectives (Gander et al. 2011; Pérez de Heredia-Torres et al. 2020). Moreover, the mental fatigue components of tools like the Multidimensional Fatigue Inventory (MFI) could provide valuable insights for monitoring and managing post-COVID fatigue, facilitating workplace adaptations such as workload adjustments and flexible scheduling (Campos et al. 2022; Mamyrbaev et al. 2023).

Among psychiatric symptoms, changes in anxiety and depression were most closely linked to shifts in fatigue characteristics (Poole-Wright et al. 2023). The worsening of fatigue correlated with increases in anxiety and depression, suggesting a shared underlying pathological mechanism, potentially involving COVID-19-induced inflammation and GABAergic impairments. Conversely, no significant associations were observed in individuals without persistent fatigue, underscoring variability in recovery mechanisms (Versace et al. 2021). Future research should explore the neural pathways and mediators contributing to distinct fatigue recovery trajectories post-COVID-19. Clinically, emphasis should be placed on monitoring neuropsychiatric symptoms in individuals with persistent fatigue. Mental fatigue, in particular, should serve as a primary target for interdisciplinary interventions integrating psychological, cognitive, and occupational therapies to optimize recovery (Pleguezuelos et al. 2023).

Several limitations warrant consideration. First, follow-up assessments were limited to three months post-infection, restricting insights into longer-term fatigue trajectories. Extended follow-ups at six months or one year are essential for a comprehensive understanding of the evolution of fatigue. Second, while we examined associations between fatigue and cognitive function, the underlying mechanisms driving these relationships remain unclear. Future research should focus on identifying potential mediators, such as inflammatory markers, neuroimaging findings, or lifestyle factors, to elucidate the biological underpinnings of post-COVID fatigue and its neuropsychiatric impacts. Third, we did not separately examine how different dimensions of fatigue relate to neurological symptoms (e.g., cognitive impairments) versus psychiatric symptoms (e.g., anxiety, depression, PTSD and sleep disorder). Future studies should consider analyzing these symptom domains independently to better understand their distinct associations with fatigue.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Post-COVID fatigue is not a standalone clinical condition; its characteristics change dynamically over time, with different dimensions exerting varying influences on neuropsychiatric symptoms throughout the recovery process. Notably, changes in mental fatigue are strongly associated with alterations in anxiety, depression, and memory problems, emphasizing its critical role in the recovery trajectory. To prevent the chronic progression of fatigue and reduce the associated neuropsychiatric burden, an integrated, multidisciplinary approach—incorporating care from physicians, occupational therapists, and psychologists, is crucial for the effective rehabilitation of individuals with post-COVID fatigue.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p:
Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.
Supplementary file1 (DOCX 703 KB)

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Wenrui Bao and Siyuan Chen contributed equally to this work.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: Xuan Niu and Yuchen Zhang contributed to the study concept and design. Ningkun Hong, Gengchen Ye, Chiyin Li, Jingmei Xie, Peng Li, and Heng Li were responsible for data collection. Yang Wang and Jixin Liu contributed to imaging analysis. Wenrui Bao and Siyuan Chen wrote the draft of the manuscript. All co-authors approved the final version of the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-82202121, the Health Research and Innovation Capacity Strengthening Platform Program of Shaanxi Province-2023PT-09, the Key Industry Innovation Chain Fund in the Social Development Field of Shaanxi Province-2024SF-ZDCYL-01-08, the Key Research and Development Plan of Xianyang City- L2023-ZDYF-SF-035 and L2022ZDYF-SF-026, and the Science and Technology Research Project of Shaanxi Nuclear Industry Group Co., Ltd-No. 61240302.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

notes.title: Conflict of interest
notes.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.
